next_steps: Review Turville-Petre's full article or comparably direct scholarship before adding personal-role vocabulary.
notes: The work-level Guido translation-adaptation relation does not settle the personal role.
openQuestions.question: Should John Clerk receive a distinct translator and/or adapter role in addition to attributed author?

next_steps: Review a direct scholarly dating argument; retain both source-attributed values until then.
openQuestions.question: How should DMER's 1350-1399 composition range and MEC's questioned ?a1400 value ultimately be normalized?

next_steps: Consult a modern codicological study; until then preserve both claims with explicit attribution.
openQuestions.question: How should MEC/LALME's two hands and Glasgow's historical one-scribe/two-styles description coexist?

next_steps: Obtain a publisher or rights-holder statement while retaining held-copy consultation, publisher-unavailable status, and volatile retailer access separately.
openQuestions.question: Who controls reuse of the 2002 Matsumoto CD-ROM transcription and color facsimile, and is any lawful public copy available?

next_steps: Obtain publisher or rights-holder terms for the original edition and CD.
Seek a copy-specific Glasgow or University Education Press record to resolve the compressed citation state.
openQuestions.question: What reuse terms govern the original 2010 Matsumoto critical edition and accompanying CD, and did Glasgow's compressed 2010 citation intend the original or revised state?

reviewNotes: Primary chain: relation:001 -> relation:002 -> relation:005 -> relation:008 -> relation:010 -> relation:011.
Corrected parallel archive paths are relation:003 from the IA package to the XML encoding and relation:004 from the same package to the TIFF facsimile; neither branch passes through the other.
The target is printed pages 1-459 including inserted pages 12* and 13*, TIFFs 75-535, and witness folios 2r-214v; edition paratext and blank page 460 are outside the work boundary.
Glasgow's zero-canvas catalog record does not establish manuscript-image access. Appointment, enquiry, and publishing-request routes remain distinct.
For the 2002 offline edition, SEENET publisher-unavailable status, Glasgow-held carrier consultation, WorldCat discovery, volatile retailer access, and unknown reuse rights are independent facts.

The original 2010 critical edition remains distinct from the revised 2010 edition, 2010 concordance, 2011 third edition, and 2002 CD-ROM; its catalog/holdings route and dated non-orderable retailer routes do not establish full text or reuse rights.
No direct lineage edge to Benoît de Sainte-Maure's Roman de Troie is asserted.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML is a copy of U-M CME encoding APE7380.0001.001, encoded from a complete TIFF facsimile of the collective Panton-Donaldson EETS edition. The archive ZIP independently contains the XML and TIFF layers in parallel branches. The edition transcribes the unique Hunter 388 witness of the Middle English translation-adaptation of Guido's Historia destructionis Troiae. Distinct 2002 diplomatic/color-facsimile and original 2010 critical editions by Hiroyuki Matsumoto provide supporting scholarly representations with separately recorded access and unresolved reuse rights.

contentParts.summary: The single cataloged Middle English alliterative Troy romance, represented by a prologue and thirty-six books.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fourteenth century

assertion.notes: MEC's questioned ante-1400 value is retained alongside, not merged into, DMER's 1350-1399 range.

extent.description: One verse work represented by a prologue and thirty-six numbered books: 37 representation sections, 249 encoded LG verse groups, and 14,044 encoded L elements. The section count does not create 37 works or 37 content items.

formStatements.description: Unrhymed alliterative verse; DMER describes an AA/AX pattern with variations.

notes: Count one intellectual work once. The prologue and thirty-six books are 37 representation sections, not 37 works or 37 count-bearing content parts.
The target is BODY DIV1 positions 2-38 (generated NODE suffixes 5-41), printed pages 1-459 including inserted pages 12* and 13*, TIFFs 75-535, and witness folios 2r-214v.
Exclude all front matter, errata, the manuscript index, blank printed page 460, notes from page 461, glossary, advertisements, and all back matter from the cataloged-work boundary.
John Clerk is an attributed author at medium confidence. The prior Sir Hugh of Eglinton/Huchown attribution is catalog history, not a current responsibility.
The Turville-Petre landing page supports bibliographic identity and indexed topics only, not the article's argument details.

next_steps: Review Turville-Petre's full article or comparably direct scholarship before adding a distinct translator or adapter role.

scope.description: Panton-Donaldson source-edition representation: coeditor of Part I and editor of Part II, covering target lines 1-14044, printed pages 1-459, and TIFFs 75-535.

summaries.value: A single Middle English alliterative romance translating and adapting Guido delle Colonne's Historia destructionis Troiae, surviving in the unique known manuscript copy Glasgow MS Hunter 388 and represented here by a prologue and thirty-six books.

The Panton-Donaldson edition's front matter, manuscript index, notes, glossary, advertisements, and blank page 460 are outside the cataloged work boundary. The prologue and thirty-six books are 37 representation sections of one work.

publication.embeddedTitle: The "Gest hystoriale" of the destruction of Troy: an alliterative romance tr. from Guido de Colonna's "Hystoria troiana." Now first ed. from the unique ms. in the Hunterian Museum, University of Glasgow, with introduction, notes, and a glossary, by... Geo. A. Panton, and David Donaldson, esq.

work.summary: A single Middle English alliterative romance translating and adapting Guido delle Colonne's Historia destructionis Troiae, surviving in the unique known manuscript copy Glasgow MS Hunter 388 and represented here by a prologue and thirty-six books.
